title: V-Model — Verification & Validation SDLC
examTip: V-Model: left side = Development phases (going down), right side = Testing phases (going up). Each development phase has a corresponding testing phase directly opposite. Requirement ↔ Acceptance Test; System Design ↔ System Test; Architecture ↔ Integration Test; Module Design ↔ Unit Test.

phases.name: Requirements Analysis
phases.description: User needs gathered → System Requirements Specification

phases.name: Architecture Design
phases.description: Module breakdown, interfaces, data flow → LLD document

phases.name: Module Design
phases.description: Detailed internal design of each module — algorithms, data structures

phases.name: CODING
phases.description: Actual implementation — source code written by developers

phases.name: Unit Testing
phases.description: Test individual modules against Module Design

phases.name: Integration Testing
phases.description: Test combined modules against Architecture Design

phases.name: System Testing
phases.description: Test complete system against System Design specs

phases.name: Acceptance Testing (UAT)
phases.description: Customer validates system against original requirements

theory:
The V-Model (Verification and Validation Model) is a sequential SDLC model that extends the Waterfall model by explicitly linking each development phase to a corresponding testing phase. The model gets its name from the V-shape formed when the development phases (left arm) and testing phases (right arm) are drawn with coding at the bottom.

Left Arm — Development (going down): Requirements Analysis produces the SRS. System Design defines the overall architecture. Architecture Design breaks down the system into modules. Module Design specifies internal logic of each module.

Bottom — Coding: Actual implementation of all modules.

Right Arm — Testing (going up): Unit Testing verifies each individual module against Module Design specs. Integration Testing verifies combined modules against Architecture Design. System Testing verifies the complete integrated system against System Design. Acceptance Testing (UAT) validates the system against original user requirements.

Key Principle — Correspondence: Every development phase has a directly corresponding test phase. Test plans and test cases are created during the corresponding development phase (not after coding), ensuring early detection of requirement gaps.

Verification vs Validation: Verification asks 'Are we building the product right?' (checking against specifications at each phase). Validation asks 'Are we building the right product?' (checking against user needs — done in UAT).

Advantages: Testing activities start early (test planning during requirements), highly structured, clear deliverables at each phase, defects found early are cheaper to fix.

Disadvantages: Like Waterfall, inflexible to changing requirements; no working prototype until late; not suitable for iterative or agile projects.
keyPoints: V-Model links each development phase to a corresponding testing phase via horizontal arrows
Left side (down): Requirements → System Design → Architecture Design → Module Design → Coding
Right side (up): Unit Test → Integration Test → System Test → Acceptance Test (UAT)
Test cases written during the development phase, not after coding — enables early defect detection
Verification = building product right (against specs); Validation = building right product (against user needs)
useCases: Safety-critical systems: avionics, medical devices, automotive — where testing rigor is mandatory
Government and defense projects with strict documentation requirements
Embedded systems development with well-defined hardware/software interfaces
